mainEntity.name: Why does my daughter lives completely in the present moment. she can't understand 'after dinner' or 'when we get home' or 'in five minutes.' everything is now or it doesn't exist. getting her to do anything she doesn't want to do is a battle because she can't see that something good is coming afterward. brushing teeth? meltdown. getting dressed? meltdown. leaving the park? complete meltdown. she doesn't understand that first we do this, then we do that. i've tried explaining, bargaining, even bribing — but words just don't stick. the words disappear as soon as i say them. she can't hold onto the promise of 'after' because she can't see it. i've tried timers but she doesn't connect the beeping to what's supposed to happen. i've tried counting down but numbers mean nothing to her. she's not trying to be difficult — she genuinely doesn't understand the sequence of events. when i say 'first shoes, then playground,' she only hears 'playground' and then melts down when we're not instantly there. it's like the concept of 'first' and 'then' as a sequence just doesn't compute. morning routines are chaos because she can't follow a sequence of steps. transitions between activities are a disaster because she can't anticipate what's coming. waiting for anything is impossible because she can't see that the wait leads to something. someone at her therapy mentioned first-then boards — that she needs to see the sequence, not just hear it. but i don't know what materials i need. i don't know how to set it up. i don't know what pictures to use or how big it should be or whether to use photos or drawings. i see these beautiful pinterest boards but they seem so complicated. what do i actually need to make this work? how do i help her understand that doing the hard thing leads to the good thing? how do i make the invisible sequence visible so she can finally cooperate without constant meltdowns?

acceptedAnswer.text: VISUAL CONTINGENCY SUPPORTS / FIRST-THEN VISUAL SCHEDULES / ANTECEDENT-BASED VISUAL INTERVENTIONS / CONTINGENCY MAPPING / VISUAL SUPPORTS FOR EXECUTIVE FUNCTION! A First-Then board is a visual support tool that shows a sequence of two events: the 'First' activity (typically a non-preferred or required task) followed by the 'Then' activity (typically a preferred activity or reward). This simple but powerful tool makes abstract concepts of sequence, time, and contingency concrete and visible. First-Then boards work by: (1) providing visual information that persists (unlike spoken words that disappear), (2) making the contingency relationship explicit ('when you do X, then you get Y'), (3) reducing reliance on verbal comprehension, (4) creating predictability about what's coming next, and (5) offering visual motivation by showing the preferred item/activity that follows the non-preferred one. First-Then boards are foundational visual supports used across settings — home, school, therapy, and community. They are particularly effective for children with autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, intellectual disability, language delays, and any child who struggles with transitions, compliance, or understanding sequences. The simplicity of the two-step format makes it accessible even for very young children or those with significant cognitive challenges, while the same principle can be extended to more complex visual schedules as the child develops. Effective First-Then boards require appropriate visual representations (objects, photos, icons, or words depending on the child's level), consistent implementation, and genuine follow-through on the 'Then' component.

mainEntity.name: What is Visual Contingency Supports / First-Then Visual Schedules / Antecedent-Based Visual Interventions / Contingency Mapping / Visual Supports for Executive Function?

acceptedAnswer.text: A First-Then board is a visual support tool that shows a sequence of two events: the 'First' activity (typically a non-preferred or required task) followed by the 'Then' activity (typically a preferred activity or reward). This simple but powerful tool makes abstract concepts of sequence, time, and contingency concrete and visible. First-Then boards work by: (1) providing visual information that persists (unlike spoken words that disappear), (2) making the contingency relationship explicit ('when you do X, then you get Y'), (3) reducing reliance on verbal comprehension, (4) creating predictability about what's coming next, and (5) offering visual motivation by showing the preferred item/activity that follows the non-preferred one. First-Then boards are foundational visual supports used across settings — home, school, therapy, and community. They are particularly effective for children with autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, intellectual disability, language delays, and any child who struggles with transitions, compliance, or understanding sequences. The simplicity of the two-step format makes it accessible even for very young children or those with significant cognitive challenges, while the same principle can be extended to more complex visual schedules as the child develops. Effective First-Then boards require appropriate visual representations (objects, photos, icons, or words depending on the child's level), consistent implementation, and genuine follow-through on the 'Then' component. Common signs include: Meltdowns during transitions between activities, Difficulty complying with non-preferred tasks, Doesn't seem to understand verbal explanations of 'later' or 'after', Lives in the present — can't conceptualize future events, Struggles to wait for preferred items or activities.

mainEntity.name: How does a First-Then Board Base (Velcro Board or Folder) help with visual contingency supports?

acceptedAnswer.text: The foundation of any First-Then system is the board itself — a portable, durable surface where visual icons can be attached and changed. The board creates a consistent, designated space for the visual sequence that the child learns to reference. A well-designed First-Then board base has two clearly defined spaces: one labeled 'First' and one labeled 'Then,' with the ability to attach and remove picture symbols. Common formats include: portable folding boards (can travel between settings), clipboard-style boards (easy to hold and transport), laminated cardstock (simple and affordable), magnetic boards (uses magnets instead of Velcro), dry-erase boards with sections (allows drawing instead of icons), and binder or folder systems (protects icons and organizes multiple boards). The key features of an effective board are: durability (will be handled frequently), portability (needs to go where the child goes), clear visual distinction between First and Then sections, and compatibility with your chosen icon system (Velcro, magnets, or pockets). Starting with a simple, well-organized board base makes implementation much easier than trying to improvise with random materials. Price: ₹200–1,500. DIY option: Creating your First-Then board base: 1) SIMPLE LAMINATED VERSION: take a piece of cardstock or cardboard; divide into two sections; write 'FIRST' on left, 'THEN' on right (or use symbols if child is pre-literate); laminate for durability; attach Velcro strips where icons will go, 2) FOLDER VERSION: use a file folder; label left inside 'First,' right inside 'Then'; add Velcro strips; folder closes to protect icons and can store extras, 3) CLIPBOARD VERSION: attach laminated First-Then card to clipboard; easy to carry and point to, 4) COOKIE SHEET VERSION: use a small metal cookie sheet; icons attach with magnets; very durable and portable, 5) DRY-ERASE VERSION: divide a small dry-erase board into two sections; draw simple pictures instead of using icons; allows quick customization, 6) SIZE CONSIDERATIONS: for young children or those who need more visual prominence, use larger boards (8.5x11 or larger); for portability and discretion with older children, use smaller formats, 7) LABELING: use words, symbols, or both depending on child's level; some children benefit from color coding (green for First, red/gold for Then), 8) VELCRO APPLICATION: rough/hook velcro goes on board; soft/loop velcro goes on icons — this way icons won't stick to fabric furniture/clothing, 9) TRAVEL KIT: create a small pouch or bag that holds the board plus commonly used icons for outings, 10) MULTIPLE BOARDS: consider having one for home, one for school, one for car/travel — consistency across settings helps learning. Safety: Ensure board has no sharp edges. If child mouths objects, ensure materials are non-toxic and icons are securely attached (not a choking hazard). Supervise use with very young children.

mainEntity.name: How does a Picture Symbol Icons (PCS, Boardmaker, Photos) help with visual contingency supports?

acceptedAnswer.text: The icons placed on a First-Then board are what communicate the actual content — what the child needs to do (First) and what they'll get (Then). The right visual representation makes the difference between a child understanding the board and being confused by it. Icon options range from concrete to abstract: real objects (most concrete — actual items or miniatures), photographs (highly concrete — photos of actual items/activities), realistic drawings or clip art (moderately concrete), Picture Communication Symbols/PCS or Boardmaker symbols (abstract but standardized), and written words (most abstract — for readers only). The key is matching the icon type to the child's developmental and cognitive level. Most children starting with First-Then boards need photographs or realistic images. Abstract symbols can be introduced as comprehension develops. Consistency is essential — use the same icon for the same activity across all contexts. Having a robust library of icons for common activities and motivating items/activities means you can quickly set up First-Then boards for any situation. Price: ₹300–3,000 (commercial sets); DIY significantly less. DIY option: Creating and organizing picture icons: 1) ASSESS LEVEL: does your child recognize photos? Can they understand simple drawings? Do they need real objects? Start at their comprehension level, 2) PHOTO ICONS: take photos of actual items and activities in your home; print 2x2 inch or similar size; laminate; add Velcro to back. Use photos of YOUR toys, YOUR bathroom, YOUR car — familiarity helps, 3) GOOGLE IMAGE ICONS: search for simple, clear images of activities; print, laminate, velcro. Ensure images are uncluttered and representative, 4) FREE PCS-STYLE ICONS: websites like ARASAAC (Aragonese Center for AAC) offer free symbol libraries that can be downloaded and printed, 5) BOARDMAKER SOFTWARE: if you have access through school or therapy, Boardmaker offers standardized PCS symbols widely used in education, 6) ICON SIZE: typically 2x2 inches for standard boards; larger for children with visual impairments or who need more visual prominence, 7) CONSISTENCY: once you choose an icon for an activity, use that SAME icon everywhere — home, school, grandma's house, 8) ORGANIZATION: store icons in a binder with clear pocket pages, categorized by type (food, activities, places, people, toys); or use a small box with dividers, 9) CORE SET: create icons for your most common routines — morning (getting dressed, brushing teeth, breakfast, shoes, backpack), transitions (car, home, school), and motivators (preferred toys, snacks, activities), 10) LAMINATION IS ESSENTIAL: icons will be handled constantly; lamination protects them and allows cleaning. REPRESENTATION TIP: ensure icons clearly show the activity — 'brushing teeth' should show a toothbrush AND teeth being brushed, not just a toothbrush sitting alone. Safety: Ensure icons are large enough not to be a choking hazard for children who mouth objects. Laminated edges should be smooth. Check regularly for wear and replace damaged icons.

mainEntity.name: How does a Velcro Dots and Strips (Industrial Strength) help with visual contingency supports?

acceptedAnswer.text: Velcro (hook-and-loop fastener) is the attachment system that makes First-Then boards interactive. Icons attach and detach easily, allowing the board to be updated for each new sequence. The quality of Velcro directly impacts usability — weak Velcro means icons fall off; overly strong Velcro means children can't remove icons independently. Industrial-strength or 'heavy-duty' Velcro provides the right balance: icons stay put but can be removed with intentional effort. Velcro comes in two forms: hook (rough/scratchy side) and loop (soft/fuzzy side). Standard practice is to put hook Velcro on the board and loop Velcro on the icons — this prevents icons from sticking to fabric surfaces. Velcro dots are convenient for individual icons; Velcro strips work well for board surfaces. Having plenty of Velcro on hand means you can quickly create new icons as needed. Self-adhesive Velcro is most convenient, but staples or glue can be used for more permanent attachment. Price: ₹150–500. DIY option: Setting up your Velcro system: 1) HOOK ON BOARD, LOOP ON ICONS: rough/hook side goes on the board surface; soft/loop side goes on the back of icons. This prevents icons from sticking to clothing, furniture, car seats, 2) INDUSTRIAL STRENGTH: use heavy-duty or industrial-strength Velcro rather than lightweight craft Velcro — it lasts longer and holds better, 3) DOTS vs. STRIPS: dots are convenient for individual icons; strips work well for board surfaces where you want flexibility in icon placement, 4) SIZE MATCHING: ensure Velcro size matches icon size — too small and icons won't stick well; too large and it's wasteful, 5) APPLICATION: for self-adhesive Velcro, clean surface with alcohol first for better adhesion; press firmly and let set before use, 6) BULK BUYING: buy Velcro in bulk rolls rather than small packages — you'll use more than you think, 7) CUTTING: cut Velcro dots/strips before applying rather than trying to cut after application, 8) REPLACEMENT: Velcro wears out over time; check and replace as needed — weak Velcro that lets icons fall off undermines the system, 9) CHILD ACCESSIBILITY: test that your child can remove icons independently if that's part of your system — too strong and they'll get frustrated, 10) COLOR COORDINATION: Velcro comes in various colors; some parents match Velcro color to board sections (green for First, blue for Then) for additional visual cueing. Safety: Small Velcro pieces can be a choking hazard. Ensure Velcro is securely attached to icons and boards. Supervise young children to prevent them from peeling off and mouthing Velcro pieces.

mainEntity.name: How does a Laminator and Laminating Pouches help with visual contingency supports?

acceptedAnswer.text: A laminator transforms fragile paper icons into durable, wipeable, long-lasting visual supports. First-Then board components get handled constantly — they're carried between rooms, pointed at, grabbed, sometimes mouthed by young children, and exposed to spills and dirt. Without lamination, paper icons quickly become torn, crumpled, and unusable. Lamination provides: durability (resists tearing and wear), protection from moisture and spills, cleanability (wipe with damp cloth), rigidity (icons are easier to handle and attach), professional appearance (looks intentional, not homemade), and longevity (icons last months or years instead of days). For families implementing visual supports, a laminator is one of the best investments — it's used not just for First-Then boards but for visual schedules, choice boards, communication supports, routine cards, and countless other tools. Pouches come in various thicknesses (measured in mil); 3-5 mil is standard for icons, with thicker options for items that need extra rigidity. Price: ₹1,500–4,000 (laminator); ₹300–800 (pouches). DIY option: Lamination best practices: 1) LAMINATOR SELECTION: choose a laminator that accommodates at least letter-size pages (A4); thermal laminators are most common for home use; cold laminators work but produce less durable results, 2) POUCH THICKNESS: 3 mil for standard icons; 5 mil for items that need extra rigidity or will be handled roughly, 3) LEAVING A BORDER: when laminating, leave a small sealed border around the image (at least 1/8 inch) — this prevents peeling and increases durability, 4) CARRIER SHEETS: some laminators work best with carrier sheets (folded paper that holds the pouch); check your model's recommendations, 5) COOLING: let laminated items cool completely before cutting or applying Velcro — cutting while hot can cause edges to separate, 6) CUTTING: round corners slightly when cutting — rounded corners are less likely to cause paper cuts and less likely to peel, 7) BATCH PROCESSING: laminate many icons at once to save time — arrange multiple small images on one page before laminating, 8) DOUBLE LAMINATION: for items that will be handled extremely roughly, you can laminate twice, 9) ALTERNATIVES IF NO LAMINATOR: clear packing tape applied to both sides (less durable but works); adhesive contact paper; clear report covers, 10) MAINTENANCE: wipe laminated icons with damp cloth when dirty; replace any icons where lamination is peeling or compromised. Safety: Laminators get very hot — keep away from children. Let laminated items cool before handling. Check that laminated edges are smooth with no sharp points.

mainEntity.name: How does a Real Object Miniatures and Tactile Items help with visual contingency supports?

acceptedAnswer.text: For some children — especially those who are very young, have significant developmental delays, or have visual impairments — two-dimensional pictures are too abstract. They need real, three-dimensional objects to understand what the icon represents. Object-based First-Then systems use miniature objects or actual items to represent activities. For example: a real toothbrush for 'brush teeth,' a small toy car for 'car ride,' a Lego piece for 'play with Legos,' a cookie for 'snack.' Objects can be attached to boards using velcro, placed in pockets, or set in designated spots. The progression from objects to pictures is developmental: children typically start by understanding real objects, then photos of objects, then realistic drawings, then abstract symbols. If a child doesn't respond to picture-based First-Then boards, stepping back to real objects often bridges the comprehension gap. Object schedules can be combined with pictures — using an object with a matching picture attached, then gradually fading the object while the picture remains. Price: ₹300–1,500 (miniatures); often free using actual items. DIY option: Implementing object-based First-Then systems: 1) ASSESS WHETHER OBJECTS ARE NEEDED: if your child doesn't respond to photos or pictures, try objects; if they do respond to pictures, objects may not be necessary, 2) REPRESENTATIONAL OBJECTS: choose objects that clearly represent the activity — real toothbrush for brushing teeth, actual shoe for putting on shoes, piece of actual snack for snack time, 3) MINIATURES: for activities that can't use real items, find miniatures — toy cars for car rides, dollhouse furniture for activities in different rooms, small figurines for 'play with toys', 4) ATTACHMENT OPTIONS: velcro strong enough to hold objects; clear pockets or pouches attached to board; small boxes or containers for each position; velcro dots on flat side of objects, 5) PAIRING WITH PHOTOS: attach a photo to each object; over time, the child learns to associate the photo with the object and can transition to photo-only systems, 6) CONSISTENCY: use the SAME object for the same activity every time; switching objects creates confusion, 7) SIZE CONSIDERATIONS: objects should be small enough to fit on the board but large enough to handle easily; not so small as to be choking hazards, 8) FADING OBJECTS TO PICTURES: once child consistently responds to object, introduce photo alongside; gradually make photo more prominent and object smaller; eventually remove object entirely, 9) TRAVEL OBJECTS: for common transitions, create a portable object kit — small bag with key objects for outings, 10) TACTILE EMPHASIS: for children with visual impairments, emphasize tactile distinctiveness — items with different textures are easier to distinguish. Safety: Miniature objects can be choking hazards. Use objects appropriately sized for the child's age and developmental level. Supervise closely with children who mouth objects. Ensure objects have no small detachable parts.

mainEntity.name: How does a Token Board or Token Economy System help with visual contingency supports?

acceptedAnswer.text: A token board extends the First-Then concept by adding a visual tracking system for earning the 'Then' reward. Instead of immediately receiving the reward after one 'First' task, the child earns tokens (stars, stickers, checkmarks, or other markers) that accumulate toward the 'Then' item. This builds delayed gratification skills, allows for multiple steps before reinforcement, and provides visible progress toward the goal. Token systems work because they: (1) make progress visible (child can see tokens accumulating), (2) break larger expectations into smaller steps, (3) teach that effort accumulates toward rewards, (4) allow for graduated delay of reinforcement, and (5) can be customized to the child's current tolerance. A child who can't wait at all might start with a one-token board (essentially First-Then). As tolerance develops, tokens can increase: 2 tokens, then 3, then 5. Token boards are particularly useful for longer routines (morning routine = 5 tokens = 5 steps = 1 reward) and for shaping behavior over time. The token board typically shows empty spaces (circles, stars, squares) and tokens are placed in each space as steps are completed. When all spaces are filled, the reward is delivered. Price: ₹300–1,500. DIY option: Creating and using token boards: 1) START SMALL: begin with 1-2 tokens (essentially First-Then or First-First-Then) before expecting 5+ tokens; build tolerance gradually, 2) BOARD DESIGN: create a strip or board with empty spaces (circles, stars, squares); typically 1-10 spaces depending on child's level; include a picture of the reward at the end, 3) TOKEN OPTIONS: stars, stickers, smiley faces, checkmarks, Velcro pieces, coins (plastic), characters the child likes — tokens should be motivating to place, 4) VISUAL CLARITY: board should clearly show 'this many tokens = this reward'; some boards show the reward picture next to the final token space, 5) CONTINGENCY: be extremely clear on what earns a token — 'Every time you [specific behavior], you get a token. When you have [number] tokens, you get [reward],' 6) IMMEDIATE TOKEN DELIVERY: when the child completes the task, give the token immediately — don't delay; token delivery should be as immediate as possible even though the reward is delayed, 7) ENTHUSIASTIC DELIVERY: 'You did it! Here's your token! You have THREE tokens now — only TWO more and you get iPad time!', 8) SHOW PROGRESS: point to the board frequently — 'Look, you have three tokens already!' — keep the visual progress salient, 9) ALWAYS DELIVER: when tokens are complete, ALWAYS deliver the reward immediately and enthusiastically; never take away earned tokens as punishment, 10) CUSTOMIZATION: some children prefer specific themes — dinosaur tokens, princess stickers, car stamps — match to interests when possible. FADING TOKENS: over time, increase the number of tokens required before reward, building delayed gratification skills. Safety: Small tokens can be choking hazards. Use appropriately sized tokens for the child's age. Do not take away earned tokens as punishment — this destroys trust in the system.

@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/visual supports / applied behavior analysis / executive function / transition support / communication / self-regulation / task completion / compliance / motivation/first-then boards / visual schedules / contingency mapping / token economies / transition strategies / task sequencing / delayed gratification / behavior support / expectation setting/9-materials-that-help-with-first-then-boards#howto
@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Visual Contingency Supports
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. First-Then boards leverage the principle of visual contingency to help children understand sequences, tolerate non-preferred activities, and delay gratification. The tool works because: (1) visual information persists while verbal information disappears, (2) the contingency relationship is made explicit and concrete, (3) the motivating 'Then' remains visible throughout the 'First,' maintaining motivation, and (4) predictability reduces anxiety about what's coming. First-Then boards are foundational visual supports that can be used with children as young as 18-24 months (with appropriate representation level) through adulthood. They are particularly effective for children with autism spectrum disorder, who often process visual information more efficiently than auditory information, and for children with ADHD, who struggle with delayed gratification and executive function. The key to success is: matching the representation level to the child (objects → photos → symbols), using genuine motivators on the 'Then' side, and absolute consistency in following through — when 'First' is complete, 'Then' ALWAYS arrives. First-Then boards can be progressively extended to visual schedules (3+ steps), token systems (multiple 'Firsts' before 'Then'), and eventually internalized as self-management strategies.

supply.name: First-Then Board Base (Velcro Board or Folder)

step.name: Understand the Problem
step.text: A First-Then board is a visual support tool that shows a sequence of two events: the 'First' activity (typically a non-preferred or required task) followed by the 'Then' activity (typically a preferred activity or reward). This simple but powerful tool makes abstract concepts of sequence, time, and contingency concrete and visible. First-Then boards work by: (1) providing visual information that persists (unlike spoken words that disappear), (2) making the contingency relationship explicit ('when you do X, then you get Y'), (3) reducing reliance on verbal comprehension, (4) creating predictability about what's coming next, and (5) offering visual motivation by showing the preferred item/activity that follows the non-preferred one. First-Then boards are foundational visual supports used across settings — home, school, therapy, and community. They are particularly effective for children with autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, intellectual disability, language delays, and any child who struggles with transitions, compliance, or understanding sequences. The simplicity of the two-step format makes it accessible even for very young children or those with significant cognitive challenges, while the same principle can be extended to more complex visual schedules as the child develops. Effective First-Then boards require appropriate visual representations (objects, photos, icons, or words depending on the child's level), consistent implementation, and genuine follow-through on the 'Then' component. First-Then boards leverage the principle of visual contingency to help children understand sequences, tolerate non-preferred activities, and delay gratification. The tool works because: (1) visual information persists while verbal information disappears, (2) the contingency relationship is made explicit and concrete, (3) the motivating 'Then' remains visible throughout the 'First,' maintaining motivation, and (4) predictability reduces anxiety about what's coming. First-Then boards are foundational visual supports that can be used with children as young as 18-24 months (with appropriate representation level) through adulthood. They are particularly effective for children with autism spectrum disorder, who often process visual information more efficiently than auditory information, and for children with ADHD, who struggle with delayed gratification and executive function. The key to success is: matching the representation level to the child (objects → photos → symbols), using genuine motivators on the 'Then' side, and absolute consistency in following through — when 'First' is complete, 'Then' ALWAYS arrives. First-Then boards can be progressively extended to visual schedules (3+ steps), token systems (multiple 'Firsts' before 'Then'), and eventually internalized as self-management strategies.

step.name: Start with Foundation Materials
step.text: Begin with: First-Then Board Base (Velcro Board or Folder), Picture Symbol Icons (PCS, Boardmaker, Photos), Velcro Dots and Strips (Industrial Strength).

signOrSymptom.name: Doesn't seem to understand verbal explanations of 'later' or 'after'
Lives in the present — can't conceptualize future events
Struggles to wait for preferred items or activities
Doesn't connect current behavior to future outcomes
Becomes upset when activities end unexpectedly

@graph.name: 9 Materials for Visual Contingency Supports
@graph.description: Evidence-based materials for ages 2–12 years (with materials adapted for developmental level; visual supports may continue into adolescence and adulthood)

item.name: First-Then Board Base (Velcro Board or Folder)
item.description: The foundation of any First-Then system is the board itself — a portable, durable surface where visual icons can be attached and changed. The board creates a consistent, designated space for the visual sequence that the child learns to reference. A well-designed First-Then board base has two clearly defined spaces: one labeled 'First' and one labeled 'Then,' with the ability to attach and remove picture symbols. Common formats include: portable folding boards (can travel between settings), clipboard-style boards (easy to hold and transport), laminated cardstock (simple and affordable), magnetic boards (uses magnets instead of Velcro), dry-erase boards with sections (allows drawing instead of icons), and binder or folder systems (protects icons and organizes multiple boards). The key features of an effective board are: durability (will be handled frequently), portability (needs to go where the child goes), clear visual distinction between First and Then sections, and compatibility with your chosen icon system (Velcro, magnets, or pockets). Starting with a simple, well-organized board base makes implementation much easier than trying to improvise with random materials.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating your First-Then board base: 1) SIMPLE LAMINATED VERSION: take a piece of cardstock or cardboard; divide into two sections; write 'FIRST' on left, 'THEN' on right (or use symbols if child is pre-literate); laminate for durability; attach Velcro strips where icons will go, 2) FOLDER VERSION: use a file folder; label left inside 'First,' right inside 'Then'; add Velcro strips; folder closes to protect icons and can store extras, 3) CLIPBOARD VERSION: attach laminated First-Then card to clipboard; easy to carry and point to, 4) COOKIE SHEET VERSION: use a small metal cookie sheet; icons attach with magnets; very durable and portable, 5) DRY-ERASE VERSION: divide a small dry-erase board into two sections; draw simple pictures instead of using icons; allows quick customization, 6) SIZE CONSIDERATIONS: for young children or those who need more visual prominence, use larger boards (8.5x11 or larger); for portability and discretion with older children, use smaller formats, 7) LABELING: use words, symbols, or both depending on child's level; some children benefit from color coding (green for First, red/gold for Then), 8) VELCRO APPLICATION: rough/hook velcro goes on board; soft/loop velcro goes on icons — this way icons won't stick to fabric furniture/clothing, 9) TRAVEL KIT: create a small pouch or bag that holds the board plus commonly used icons for outings, 10) MULTIPLE BOARDS: consider having one for home, one for school, one for car/travel — consistency across settings helps learning.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure board has no sharp edges. If child mouths objects, ensure materials are non-toxic and icons are securely attached (not a choking hazard). Supervise use with very young children.

item.name: Picture Symbol Icons (PCS, Boardmaker, Photos)
item.description: The icons placed on a First-Then board are what communicate the actual content — what the child needs to do (First) and what they'll get (Then). The right visual representation makes the difference between a child understanding the board and being confused by it. Icon options range from concrete to abstract: real objects (most concrete — actual items or miniatures), photographs (highly concrete — photos of actual items/activities), realistic drawings or clip art (moderately concrete), Picture Communication Symbols/PCS or Boardmaker symbols (abstract but standardized), and written words (most abstract — for readers only). The key is matching the icon type to the child's developmental and cognitive level. Most children starting with First-Then boards need photographs or realistic images. Abstract symbols can be introduced as comprehension develops. Consistency is essential — use the same icon for the same activity across all contexts. Having a robust library of icons for common activities and motivating items/activities means you can quickly set up First-Then boards for any situation.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating and organizing picture icons: 1) ASSESS LEVEL: does your child recognize photos? Can they understand simple drawings? Do they need real objects? Start at their comprehension level, 2) PHOTO ICONS: take photos of actual items and activities in your home; print 2x2 inch or similar size; laminate; add Velcro to back. Use photos of YOUR toys, YOUR bathroom, YOUR car — familiarity helps, 3) GOOGLE IMAGE ICONS: search for simple, clear images of activities; print, laminate, velcro. Ensure images are uncluttered and representative, 4) FREE PCS-STYLE ICONS: websites like ARASAAC (Aragonese Center for AAC) offer free symbol libraries that can be downloaded and printed, 5) BOARDMAKER SOFTWARE: if you have access through school or therapy, Boardmaker offers standardized PCS symbols widely used in education, 6) ICON SIZE: typically 2x2 inches for standard boards; larger for children with visual impairments or who need more visual prominence, 7) CONSISTENCY: once you choose an icon for an activity, use that SAME icon everywhere — home, school, grandma's house, 8) ORGANIZATION: store icons in a binder with clear pocket pages, categorized by type (food, activities, places, people, toys); or use a small box with dividers, 9) CORE SET: create icons for your most common routines — morning (getting dressed, brushing teeth, breakfast, shoes, backpack), transitions (car, home, school), and motivators (preferred toys, snacks, activities), 10) LAMINATION IS ESSENTIAL: icons will be handled constantly; lamination protects them and allows cleaning. REPRESENTATION TIP: ensure icons clearly show the activity — 'brushing teeth' should show a toothbrush AND teeth being brushed, not just a toothbrush sitting alone.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure icons are large enough not to be a choking hazard for children who mouth objects. Laminated edges should be smooth. Check regularly for wear and replace damaged icons.

item.name: Velcro Dots and Strips (Industrial Strength)
item.description: Velcro (hook-and-loop fastener) is the attachment system that makes First-Then boards interactive. Icons attach and detach easily, allowing the board to be updated for each new sequence. The quality of Velcro directly impacts usability — weak Velcro means icons fall off; overly strong Velcro means children can't remove icons independently. Industrial-strength or 'heavy-duty' Velcro provides the right balance: icons stay put but can be removed with intentional effort. Velcro comes in two forms: hook (rough/scratchy side) and loop (soft/fuzzy side). Standard practice is to put hook Velcro on the board and loop Velcro on the icons — this prevents icons from sticking to fabric surfaces. Velcro dots are convenient for individual icons; Velcro strips work well for board surfaces. Having plenty of Velcro on hand means you can quickly create new icons as needed. Self-adhesive Velcro is most convenient, but staples or glue can be used for more permanent attachment.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Setting up your Velcro system: 1) HOOK ON BOARD, LOOP ON ICONS: rough/hook side goes on the board surface; soft/loop side goes on the back of icons. This prevents icons from sticking to clothing, furniture, car seats, 2) INDUSTRIAL STRENGTH: use heavy-duty or industrial-strength Velcro rather than lightweight craft Velcro — it lasts longer and holds better, 3) DOTS vs. STRIPS: dots are convenient for individual icons; strips work well for board surfaces where you want flexibility in icon placement, 4) SIZE MATCHING: ensure Velcro size matches icon size — too small and icons won't stick well; too large and it's wasteful, 5) APPLICATION: for self-adhesive Velcro, clean surface with alcohol first for better adhesion; press firmly and let set before use, 6) BULK BUYING: buy Velcro in bulk rolls rather than small packages — you'll use more than you think, 7) CUTTING: cut Velcro dots/strips before applying rather than trying to cut after application, 8) REPLACEMENT: Velcro wears out over time; check and replace as needed — weak Velcro that lets icons fall off undermines the system, 9) CHILD ACCESSIBILITY: test that your child can remove icons independently if that's part of your system — too strong and they'll get frustrated, 10) COLOR COORDINATION: Velcro comes in various colors; some parents match Velcro color to board sections (green for First, blue for Then) for additional visual cueing.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Small Velcro pieces can be a choking hazard. Ensure Velcro is securely attached to icons and boards. Supervise young children to prevent them from peeling off and mouthing Velcro pieces.

item.name: Laminator and Laminating Pouches
item.description: A laminator transforms fragile paper icons into durable, wipeable, long-lasting visual supports. First-Then board components get handled constantly — they're carried between rooms, pointed at, grabbed, sometimes mouthed by young children, and exposed to spills and dirt. Without lamination, paper icons quickly become torn, crumpled, and unusable. Lamination provides: durability (resists tearing and wear), protection from moisture and spills, cleanability (wipe with damp cloth), rigidity (icons are easier to handle and attach), professional appearance (looks intentional, not homemade), and longevity (icons last months or years instead of days). For families implementing visual supports, a laminator is one of the best investments — it's used not just for First-Then boards but for visual schedules, choice boards, communication supports, routine cards, and countless other tools. Pouches come in various thicknesses (measured in mil); 3-5 mil is standard for icons, with thicker options for items that need extra rigidity.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Lamination best practices: 1) LAMINATOR SELECTION: choose a laminator that accommodates at least letter-size pages (A4); thermal laminators are most common for home use; cold laminators work but produce less durable results, 2) POUCH THICKNESS: 3 mil for standard icons; 5 mil for items that need extra rigidity or will be handled roughly, 3) LEAVING A BORDER: when laminating, leave a small sealed border around the image (at least 1/8 inch) — this prevents peeling and increases durability, 4) CARRIER SHEETS: some laminators work best with carrier sheets (folded paper that holds the pouch); check your model's recommendations, 5) COOLING: let laminated items cool completely before cutting or applying Velcro — cutting while hot can cause edges to separate, 6) CUTTING: round corners slightly when cutting — rounded corners are less likely to cause paper cuts and less likely to peel, 7) BATCH PROCESSING: laminate many icons at once to save time — arrange multiple small images on one page before laminating, 8) DOUBLE LAMINATION: for items that will be handled extremely roughly, you can laminate twice, 9) ALTERNATIVES IF NO LAMINATOR: clear packing tape applied to both sides (less durable but works); adhesive contact paper; clear report covers, 10) MAINTENANCE: wipe laminated icons with damp cloth when dirty; replace any icons where lamination is peeling or compromised.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Laminators get very hot — keep away from children. Let laminated items cool before handling. Check that laminated edges are smooth with no sharp points.

item.name: Real Object Miniatures and Tactile Items
item.description: For some children — especially those who are very young, have significant developmental delays, or have visual impairments — two-dimensional pictures are too abstract. They need real, three-dimensional objects to understand what the icon represents. Object-based First-Then systems use miniature objects or actual items to represent activities. For example: a real toothbrush for 'brush teeth,' a small toy car for 'car ride,' a Lego piece for 'play with Legos,' a cookie for 'snack.' Objects can be attached to boards using velcro, placed in pockets, or set in designated spots. The progression from objects to pictures is developmental: children typically start by understanding real objects, then photos of objects, then realistic drawings, then abstract symbols. If a child doesn't respond to picture-based First-Then boards, stepping back to real objects often bridges the comprehension gap. Object schedules can be combined with pictures — using an object with a matching picture attached, then gradually fading the object while the picture remains.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Implementing object-based First-Then systems: 1) ASSESS WHETHER OBJECTS ARE NEEDED: if your child doesn't respond to photos or pictures, try objects; if they do respond to pictures, objects may not be necessary, 2) REPRESENTATIONAL OBJECTS: choose objects that clearly represent the activity — real toothbrush for brushing teeth, actual shoe for putting on shoes, piece of actual snack for snack time, 3) MINIATURES: for activities that can't use real items, find miniatures — toy cars for car rides, dollhouse furniture for activities in different rooms, small figurines for 'play with toys', 4) ATTACHMENT OPTIONS: velcro strong enough to hold objects; clear pockets or pouches attached to board; small boxes or containers for each position; velcro dots on flat side of objects, 5) PAIRING WITH PHOTOS: attach a photo to each object; over time, the child learns to associate the photo with the object and can transition to photo-only systems, 6) CONSISTENCY: use the SAME object for the same activity every time; switching objects creates confusion, 7) SIZE CONSIDERATIONS: objects should be small enough to fit on the board but large enough to handle easily; not so small as to be choking hazards, 8) FADING OBJECTS TO PICTURES: once child consistently responds to object, introduce photo alongside; gradually make photo more prominent and object smaller; eventually remove object entirely, 9) TRAVEL OBJECTS: for common transitions, create a portable object kit — small bag with key objects for outings, 10) TACTILE EMPHASIS: for children with visual impairments, emphasize tactile distinctiveness — items with different textures are easier to distinguish.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Miniature objects can be choking hazards. Use objects appropriately sized for the child's age and developmental level. Supervise closely with children who mouth objects. Ensure objects have no small detachable parts.

item.name: Token Board or Token Economy System
item.description: A token board extends the First-Then concept by adding a visual tracking system for earning the 'Then' reward. Instead of immediately receiving the reward after one 'First' task, the child earns tokens (stars, stickers, checkmarks, or other markers) that accumulate toward the 'Then' item. This builds delayed gratification skills, allows for multiple steps before reinforcement, and provides visible progress toward the goal. Token systems work because they: (1) make progress visible (child can see tokens accumulating), (2) break larger expectations into smaller steps, (3) teach that effort accumulates toward rewards, (4) allow for graduated delay of reinforcement, and (5) can be customized to the child's current tolerance. A child who can't wait at all might start with a one-token board (essentially First-Then). As tolerance develops, tokens can increase: 2 tokens, then 3, then 5. Token boards are particularly useful for longer routines (morning routine = 5 tokens = 5 steps = 1 reward) and for shaping behavior over time. The token board typically shows empty spaces (circles, stars, squares) and tokens are placed in each space as steps are completed. When all spaces are filled, the reward is delivered.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating and using token boards: 1) START SMALL: begin with 1-2 tokens (essentially First-Then or First-First-Then) before expecting 5+ tokens; build tolerance gradually, 2) BOARD DESIGN: create a strip or board with empty spaces (circles, stars, squares); typically 1-10 spaces depending on child's level; include a picture of the reward at the end, 3) TOKEN OPTIONS: stars, stickers, smiley faces, checkmarks, Velcro pieces, coins (plastic), characters the child likes — tokens should be motivating to place, 4) VISUAL CLARITY: board should clearly show 'this many tokens = this reward'; some boards show the reward picture next to the final token space, 5) CONTINGENCY: be extremely clear on what earns a token — 'Every time you [specific behavior], you get a token. When you have [number] tokens, you get [reward],' 6) IMMEDIATE TOKEN DELIVERY: when the child completes the task, give the token immediately — don't delay; token delivery should be as immediate as possible even though the reward is delayed, 7) ENTHUSIASTIC DELIVERY: 'You did it! Here's your token! You have THREE tokens now — only TWO more and you get iPad time!', 8) SHOW PROGRESS: point to the board frequently — 'Look, you have three tokens already!' — keep the visual progress salient, 9) ALWAYS DELIVER: when tokens are complete, ALWAYS deliver the reward immediately and enthusiastically; never take away earned tokens as punishment, 10) CUSTOMIZATION: some children prefer specific themes — dinosaur tokens, princess stickers, car stamps — match to interests when possible. FADING TOKENS: over time, increase the number of tokens required before reward, building delayed gratification skills.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Small tokens can be choking hazards. Use appropriately sized tokens for the child's age. Do not take away earned tokens as punishment — this destroys trust in the system.

item.name: Visual Timer (Sand Timer, Time Timer, App)
item.description: Time is invisible and abstract — children who struggle with First-Then often also struggle with 'wait' and 'how much longer.' Visual timers make time visible by showing time passing in a concrete, visual way. For First-Then boards, visual timers serve multiple purposes: (1) showing how long the 'First' activity will last ('First homework for 10 minutes'), (2) showing how long before the 'Then' activity ends ('Then 5 minutes of iPad'), and (3) preparing for transitions ('When the timer is done, we're moving to the next thing'). Common visual timer types include: sand timers (very concrete — watch sand fall), Time Timer brand (shows red disk shrinking as time passes), digital timers with visual display, and timer apps on phones/tablets. The Time Timer is particularly effective because it shows the quantity of time remaining as a visual space that shrinks — children don't need to understand numbers to see 'this much time left.' Visual timers reduce 'are we done yet?' questions and power struggles about ending preferred activities. When combined with First-Then boards, they provide complete visual information: WHAT is happening and HOW LONG it will happen.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Using visual timers with First-Then boards: 1) CHOOSE APPROPRIATE TIMER: sand timers for very young children or those who need most concrete representation; Time Timer for most children; apps for convenience and portability, 2) TIME TIMER: the Time Timer brand (or similar visual timers) shows a red disk that shrinks as time passes — no number reading required; available in 3, 12, and 60-minute versions, 3) SAND TIMERS: come in various durations (1, 3, 5 minutes typically); very concrete and engaging; multiple colors often available, 4) PAIR WITH FIRST-THEN: 'First we do homework until the timer is done, THEN you get iPad time' — timer goes next to the 'First' icon, 5) TIMER FOR 'THEN' ALSO: set timer for how long the preferred activity lasts — this prepares for the transition to the next 'First'; 'You have iPad until the timer is done,' 6) START SMALL: if child has very limited waiting tolerance, start with very short timers (1-2 minutes) and build up, 7) PREDICTABLE USE: use timer consistently — child learns that timer = reliable predictor of transitions, 8) NO EXTENDING: when timer ends, activity ends (or transitions happen); don't repeatedly reset timer as this undermines its predictive value, 9) TIMER VISIBILITY: place timer where child can easily see it; point to it: 'Look, just a little time left,' 10) APPS: phone/tablet apps include Time Timer app, visual timer apps, and Alexa/Google Home timer voice commands for auditory learners. TRANSITION SUPPORT: some timers make a sound when complete; pair sound with transition routine so child learns sound = time to change.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Glass sand timers can break — choose plastic versions for young children. Ensure timer is placed where child can see but not grab and throw. Some children become distressed watching time run out — introduce gradually.

item.name: Portable Mini First-Then Card/Keychain
item.description: First-Then boards need to go wherever the child goes — and sometimes a full board isn't practical. Portable mini First-Then systems provide the same visual support in a compact, take-anywhere format. These include: keychain cards (small First-Then cards on a ring), lanyard systems (worn around adult's neck for constant access), wallet-sized cards, wristband visuals, or small flip books. Portability is essential because transitions and non-preferred tasks don't just happen at home — they happen at the grocery store, at grandma's house, in the car, at the doctor's office, at the playground. Having a portable system means visual support is always available. Some families maintain a full First-Then board at home and a portable version for outings. Others use exclusively portable systems for simplicity. The key is that the system is always accessible — if you need it and it's at home, it doesn't help. Portable systems often use photographs or simple symbols and include icons for common transitions and motivating items.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating portable First-Then systems: 1) KEYCHAIN CARDS: create small (2x3 inch) First-Then cards; laminate heavily; hole punch corner; attach to ring or carabiner that clips to your bag, 2) LANYARD SYSTEM: same small cards attached to lanyard worn around your neck — always visible and accessible, 3) VELCRO FLIP BOOK: small notebook with Velcro inside; flip pages for First and Then; carry interchangeable mini icons, 4) PHOTO OPTIONS: take photos of common 'Then' rewards (car snack, playground, home, TV, toy) and common 'First' tasks (shopping cart, waiting, walking); these become your portable icon library, 5) BLANK CARD: include one blank First-Then card with a dry-erase surface — you can quickly draw simple pictures for unexpected situations, 6) CAR KIT: keep a small First-Then kit in the car — board plus common icons for car-related transitions (going to store, going home, stopping for food), 7) DIAPER BAG/BACKPACK: maintain a First-Then kit in your go-bag — always there when you need it, 8) COMMUNICATION BOOK: if child uses a communication book or device, include First-Then functionality there, 9) DIGITAL OPTION: First-Then app on phone — always with you; can add photos on the fly, 10) SIMPLEST OPTION: even paper and marker in a pinch — quickly draw 'First [picture]' and 'Then [picture]'; imperfect but functional. COMMUNITY USE: 'First we walk through the store, THEN you get a cookie' — show card at store entrance and reference throughout trip.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Small cards can be lost — have backups. Keychain rings should not be small enough to be a choking hazard. Lanyards around adult necks only — never around child's neck due to strangulation risk.

item.name: Reinforcer Inventory and Choice Board
item.description: The 'Then' side of the First-Then board is only motivating if the child actually wants what's pictured there. A reinforcer inventory is a systematic assessment and collection of items, activities, and events that are genuinely motivating to the specific child. A choice board allows the child to select their 'Then' from options, increasing motivation and teaching choice-making skills. Effective reinforcers are highly individual — what motivates one child may be meaningless to another. Categories include: edibles (specific snacks, treats), tangibles (toys, objects), activities (iPad, playground, specific games), sensory (swinging, deep pressure, bubbles), social (praise, tickles, specific games with adult), and special interests (related to child's preferred topics). A reinforcer choice board typically shows 2-4 options; the child points to or selects which 'Then' they're working for. This increases investment because the child has chosen their goal. Regular preference assessments ensure you know what's currently motivating — children's preferences change over time, and yesterday's favorite might be today's rejection.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Building a reinforcer system: 1) PREFERENCE ASSESSMENT: systematically present items/activities and observe which the child consistently approaches, engages with, or asks for; note high-preference versus moderate-preference items, 2) CATEGORIES TO EXPLORE: edibles (crackers, chips, candy, juice boxes, fruit snacks); tangibles (specific toys, fidgets, objects); activities (iPad games, YouTube videos, playground, jumping, specific books); sensory (bubbles, playdough, swinging, deep pressure, spinning); social (tickles, chase games, rough play, praise), 3) OBSERVE NATURALLY: what does your child seek out? What do they ask for? What do they refuse to stop doing? These are likely reinforcers, 4) REGULAR UPDATES: preferences change; reassess monthly or when motivation seems to drop; rotate reinforcers to prevent satiation (getting tired of the same thing), 5) CHOICE BOARD: create a board with 2-4 high-preference options; child points to select which 'Then' they're working for; this increases investment and motivation, 6) MATCH TO TASK: bigger/harder tasks might warrant higher-value reinforcers; small tasks can use moderate reinforcers, 7) AVAILABILITY: some reinforcers should be available ONLY through the First-Then system — if iPad is freely available all day, it's not motivating as a 'Then,' 8) QUICK ACCESS: have reinforcers ready to deliver immediately when the 'Then' arrives; don't make the child wait after completing 'First', 9) SATIATION AWARENESS: if a child has been eating crackers all day, crackers won't be motivating; rotate and restrict access to maintain value, 10) VISUAL STORAGE: keep reinforcer icons organized and easily accessible so you can quickly update the 'Then' side of the board. CHOICE MAKING: even if you're limiting options, giving ANY choice increases motivation — 'First clean up, then... you choose: iPad or bubbles?'

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Food reinforcers should consider allergies and nutritional implications. Don't restrict access to basic needs (food, water, bathroom) as contingent reinforcers. High-value items should still be available sometimes non-contingently to prevent deprivation.

socialMedia.caption: When your child can't see past NOW to understand NEXT — words aren't enough. They need to SEE the sequence. Here are 9 materials for First-Then boards that actually work: the board base, picture icons, Velcro system, laminator, real objects for when pictures are too abstract, token boards, visual timers, portable systems for on-the-go, and reinforcer inventories. From 'NOW NOW NOW' to 'First... Then.' Making the invisible sequence visible. 💜 #FirstThenBoard #VisualSupports #AutismSupport #PinnacleBlooms
